apprentice.questionText: When sizing branch-circuit conductors for a single motor, which current value is generally used?
options: The Full-Load Current (FLC) from the NEC Tables
apprentice.explanation: To determine the ampacity of conductors, switches, and branch-circuit short-circuit and ground-fault protection, the values given in the NEC Tables (like Table 430.250) shall be used instead of the actual current rating marked on the motor nameplate.
apprentice.sparkyTip: Use the NEC tables for sizing your wire and breakers, but always use the actual nameplate rating for sizing the thermal overloads.

apprentice.questionText: Branch-circuit conductors supplying a single continuous-duty motor must have an ampacity of not less than what percentage of the motor's full-load current rating?
apprentice.explanation: Conductors that supply a single motor used in a continuous duty application must have an ampacity of not less than 125 percent of the motor's full-load current rating.
apprentice.sparkyTip: That extra 25% provides a buffer to handle the heat generated by starting current without degrading the wire insulation over time.

apprentice.questionText: Generators must be equipped with a disconnect that simultaneously opens all associated ungrounded conductors. This disconnect must be...
options: Rated for 200% of the generator output
apprentice.explanation: The disconnecting means for a generator must be lockable in the open (OFF) position in accordance with 110.25 to ensure the safety of personnel working on the system.
apprentice.sparkyTip: Lockout/Tagout saves lives! The disconnect needs to be lockable in the open position to guarantee the power stays off while you work.

apprentice.questionText: According to NEC Article 430, what is the definition of a motor controller?
options: Any switch or device that is normally used to start and stop a motor
The main breaker in the electrical service panel
The remote keypad for a Variable Frequency Drive
apprentice.explanation: A controller is defined by the NEC as any switch or device that is normally used to start and stop a motor by making and breaking the motor circuit current.
apprentice.sparkyTip: A 'controller' is just the formal NEC term for whatever device—like a contactor or manual motor switch—actually turns the motor on and off.

apprentice.questionText: When calculating the feeder conductor size for multiple motors, you take the sum of the full-load currents of all motors and add what percentage of the highest rated motor in the group?
apprentice.explanation: Conductors supplying several motors must have an ampacity of not less than the sum of the full-load current rating of all the motors, plus 25 percent of the highest rated motor in the group.
apprentice.sparkyTip: You only apply the extra 25% buffer to the LARGEST motor in the group. The rest of the motors are just added in at 100%.

apprentice.questionText: The ampacity of the conductors from the generator terminals to the first distribution device containing overcurrent protection shall not be less than what percentage of the nameplate current rating of the generator?
apprentice.explanation: The ampacity of the conductors from the generator to the first overcurrent device shall not be less than 115 percent of the nameplate current rating of the generator.
apprentice.sparkyTip: Remember the difference! Generators require 115% for conductor sizing, unlike single continuous-duty motors which use the 125% rule.

journeyman.questionText: A 25 HP, 460V, 3-phase squirrel cage motor has a nameplate FLA of 32A. Per NEC Table 430.250, the FLC is 34A. What current value must be used to size the branch circuit conductors?
journeyman.explanation: Per NEC 430.22, branch circuit conductors must have ampacity not less than 125% of the motor FLC from Table 430.250 — NOT the nameplate FLA. 34A × 1.25 = 42.5A minimum ampacity required.
journeyman.sparkyTip: Never use nameplate FLA for conductor sizing — always use Table 430.250 FLC × 125%!

journeyman.questionText: A 50 HP, 460V, 3-phase AC motor has a full-load current of 65A per Table 430.250. It is NOT a Design B energy-efficient or premium efficiency motor. If the starting current causes nuisance tripping at the standard setting, what is the absolute maximum permitted setting for an instantaneous trip circuit breaker (MCP)?
journeyman.explanation: Step 1: Standard Table Rule. Table 430.52 normally limits an instantaneous trip breaker (MCP) to 800% of the FLC (65A × 8 = 520A). Step 2: The Exception. Because the standard setting isn't holding the starting current, 430.52(C)(3) Exception No. 1 allows you to increase the setting to a maximum of 1300% for standard motors. 65A × 13 = 845A.
journeyman.sparkyTip: Watch your motor types! For standard motors, the absolute max MCP setting is 1300%. But if the question says it IS a 'Design B energy-efficient' or 'Design E' motor, the code lets you crank that breaker all the way up to 1700%!

journeyman.questionText: A 30 HP, 460V, 3-phase motor has a NEMA Code Letter K stamped on its nameplate. According to NEC Table 430.7(B), what is the maximum locked-rotor current (LRA) for this motor?
journeyman.explanation: Step 1: Find the kVA/HP range. Per Table 430.7(B), Code Letter K is 8.0 to 8.99 kVA/HP. Step 2: Use the maximum value (8.99) to find the worst-case scenario. Step 3: Apply the 3-phase formula: LRA = (HP × kVA/HP × 1000) / (Volts × 1.732). Step 4: Calculate: (30 × 8.99 × 1000) / (460 × 1.732) = 269,700 / 796.72 = 338.5A. This rounds up to 339A.
journeyman.sparkyTip: When an exam asks for Locked-Rotor Current based on a Code Letter, always calculate using the HIGHEST number in that letter's range (like 8.99 for K). You always have to plan for the worst-case inrush current!

journeyman.questionText: A 7.5 HP, 208V, 3-phase motor has an FLC of 24.2A per Table 430.250. The motor nameplate shows 22A FLA and a service factor of 1.0. What is the maximum overload protection?
journeyman.explanation: Per NEC 430.32(A)(1), motors with service factor less than 1.15 (or temp rise over 40°C) can have overload protection up to 115% of nameplate FLA. 22A × 1.15 = 25.3A maximum. Note: Use NAMEPLATE FLA for overloads, not Table FLC!
journeyman.sparkyTip: SF < 1.15 means 115% overload max. Also, always use NAMEPLATE amps for overload sizing — one of the few times you don't use Table 430.250!

journeyman.questionText: What is the maximum size standard inverse-time circuit breaker that can be used for branch-circuit, short-circuit, and ground-fault protection for a 25 HP, 460V, 3-phase, squirrel-cage motor (Design B)?
journeyman.explanation: First, find the FLC from Table 430.250 for a 25 HP, 460V motor, which is 34A. According to Table 430.52, the maximum percentage for an inverse-time breaker is 250% of the FLC. So, 34A × 2.50 = 85A. However, 85A is not a standard circuit breaker size listed in 240.6(A). Per 430.52(C)(1) Exception No. 1, if the calculated value does not correspond to a standard size, you are permitted to use the next higher standard size. The next standard size up from 85A is 90A.
journeyman.sparkyTip: The 'next size up' rule for motor branch-circuit protection is a crucial exception. Don't just stop at the calculated value; always check if you need to round up to the next standard breaker or fuse size.

journeyman.questionText: A feeder supplies three motors with full-load currents of 44 A, 26 A, and 14 A. Per 430.24, what is the minimum feeder conductor ampacity?
journeyman.explanation: Feeder ampacity = 125% of largest (44 A) + 100% of others = 95 A.
journeyman.sparkyTip: Largest motor at 125%, all others at 100%.

journeyman.questionText: A 60A circuit breaker protects a motor branch circuit. The motor control circuit is tapped from the load side of this breaker using 14 AWG copper wire and runs 20 feet through EMT to a remote Start/Stop push-button station. According to Table 430.72(B)(2), does this 14 AWG control circuit require separate overcurrent protection (inline fuses)?
options: Yes, because the 60A breaker exceeds the 45A limit for control wires extending beyond the enclosure.
No, because 14 AWG control wires are permitted to be protected by a breaker up to 100A.
Yes, because all tapped control circuits must be protected by 15-amp fuses.
No, because the remote station is within 50 feet of the controller.
journeyman.explanation: Step 1: Check the wire size and location. The wire is 14 AWG and extends beyond the motor control enclosure. Step 2: Use Table 430.72(B)(2). Find the row for 14 AWG. Because the wire leaves the enclosure, you must use Column C, which limits the maximum branch-circuit protective device to 45A. Step 3: Compare. Since the actual motor breaker is 60A (which is larger than 45A), it cannot legally protect the control circuit. Separate inline fuses must be installed.
journeyman.sparkyTip: Location is everything! If that 14 AWG wire stayed strictly INSIDE the starter box (like a reset button on the cover), you'd use Column B (100A limit), and the 60A breaker would be perfectly legal without extra fuses. But the second that wire leaves the box, you drop to Column C (45A limit) and have to add fuses to prevent a meltdown!

journeyman.questionText: A 15 HP, 460V, 3-phase motor has an FLA of 21A. The motor has a 1.15 service factor. What is the maximum overload protection setting?
journeyman.explanation: Per NEC 430.32(A)(1), motors with service factor ≥ 1.15 can have overload protection up to 125% of nameplate FLA. Using the formula FLA: 21A × 1.25 = 26.25A.
journeyman.sparkyTip: Service factor 1.15+ or 40°C rise = 125% overload. Otherwise it's 115%. This protects the motor windings!

journeyman.questionText: Where must a disconnecting means for a motor generally be located in relation to the motor location?
options: Within sight (visible and not more than 50 feet away)
At the main electrical service panel only
journeyman.explanation: The NEC requires the disconnecting means to be located in sight from the motor location and the driven machinery location. 'In sight from' means it is visible and not more than 50 feet distant.
journeyman.sparkyTip: 'Within sight' is a critical NEC definition to memorize. It always means visible AND within 50 feet!

journeyman.questionText: For a continuous-duty motor marked with a service factor of 1.15, the separate overload device shall be sized at no more than what percentage of the nameplate full-load current rating?
journeyman.explanation: Motors with a marked service factor of 1.15 or greater, or a temperature rise of 40°C or less, are allowed to have their overload devices sized at 125% of the nameplate full-load current.
journeyman.sparkyTip: Motors with a 1.15 service factor or 40°C temp rise get 125%. All other typical motors get 115% for overload sizing.

journeyman.questionText: Under which of the following conditions must the frames of stationary motors be grounded?
options: If located in a wet location and not isolated
If operating with any terminal at over 150 volts to ground
journeyman.explanation: The NEC requires the frames of stationary motors to be grounded under all of these conditions to prevent electrical shock hazards.
journeyman.sparkyTip: When in doubt, ground it out. The NEC requires equipment grounding for stationary motors in almost all typical industrial and commercial scenarios.

journeyman.questionText: On a motor nameplate, what does the 'Code Letter' indicate?
options: The temperature rating of the insulation
The physical frame size of the motor
journeyman.explanation: A code letter indicates the motor's locked-rotor kilovolt-amperes (kVA) per horsepower. This helps in determining the required short-circuit and ground-fault protection.
journeyman.sparkyTip: The code letter helps you calculate the massive inrush current the motor pulls the exact moment it tries to start turning.

master.questionText: What is the maximum inverse time circuit breaker size for a 40A FLC squirrel cage motor with no code letter?
master.explanation: Per NEC Table 430.52, inverse time breakers for squirrel cage motors without code letter can be sized up to 250% of FLC. 40A × 2.50 = 100A. If 100A doesn't hold, you may go to the next standard size per 430.52(C)(1).
master.sparkyTip: Inverse time breaker = 250% max for standard squirrel cage motors. Round UP to standard size if needed.

master.questionText: A motor feeder supplies three motors: 28A FLC, 40A FLC, and 14A FLC. What is the minimum feeder ampacity?
master.explanation: Per NEC 430.24, feeder ampacity = 125% of largest motor FLC + 100% of all other motors. (40 × 1.25) + 28 + 14 = 50 + 28 + 14 = 92A.
master.sparkyTip: Feeder = 125% of LARGEST motor + sum of all others at 100%. Always identify the biggest motor first!

master.questionText: A 10 HP, 230V, 3-phase squirrel-cage motor is installed using a dual-element (time-delay) fuse for short-circuit and ground-fault protection. What is the maximum standard size fuse permitted?
master.explanation: Step 1: Find the FLC in Table 430.250. For a 10 HP, 230V, 3-phase motor, the FLC is 28A. Step 2: Determine the multiplier for a dual-element fuse from Table 430.52, which is 175%. Step 3: Calculate: 28A × 1.75 = 49A. Step 4: Per 430.52(C)(1) Exception 1, you may round up to the next standard size. Per 240.6, the next standard fuse size is 50A.
master.sparkyTip: Don't use the nameplate! For calculations, the NEC says you MUST use the FLC tables (430.247-250) unless the motor is specifically rated for low speed or high torque.

master.questionText: A motor feeder supplies two 20 HP motors and one 10 HP motor, all 460V, 3-phase. If the branch circuits are protected by inverse-time circuit breakers, what is the maximum standard size feeder overcurrent protective device permitted?
master.explanation: Step 1: Find FLCs in Table 430.250. 20 HP = 27A; 10 HP = 14A. Step 2: Calculate the largest branch-circuit OCPD per 430.52. 27A × 250% = 67.5A. The next standard size is 70A. Step 3: Calculate the feeder maximum per 430.62(A) by adding the largest branch-circuit OCPD (70A) to the FLCs of the *other* motors (one 20 HP and one 10 HP). 70A + 27A + 14A = 111A. Step 4: Per 430.62, you must NOT exceed this value, so you must round down to the next standard size. According to Table 240.6(A), the next standard size down from 111A is 110A.
master.sparkyTip: Here is the golden rule: Branch circuits let you round UP (430.52), but Feeders force you to round DOWN (430.62). If you land on 111A, a 110A breaker is the absolute limit!

master.questionText: A 480V feeder supplies a 30 HP, 3-phase, squirrel-cage motor (Design B), a 10 HP, 3-phase, squirrel-cage motor, and a continuous 40A non-motor lighting load. What is the minimum required ampacity for the feeder conductors?
master.explanation:
First, determine the Full-Load Currents (FLC) from Table 430.250: The 30 HP motor is 40A, and the 10 HP motor is 14A. 

Per 430.24, size the motor load by taking 125% of the largest motor plus the sum of the others: (40A × 1.25) + 14A = 64A. 

Next, per 215.2(A)(1), add the continuous non-motor load at 125%: 40A × 1.25 = 50A. 

Finally, sum the two calculated values: 64A (motors) + 50A (lighting) = 114A.
master.sparkyTip: Don't just add the raw FLCs (40A + 14A = 54A)! You MUST multiply the largest motor by 125% first (50A + 14A = 64A). Then, calculate the continuous load separately (40A × 1.25 = 50A). It helps to think of this as two separate '125% rules' that you add together at the end.

master.questionText: A 460V, 3-phase feeder supplies two 25 HP Design B motors and one 50 HP wound-rotor motor. What is the maximum standard size inverse-time circuit breaker permitted for feeder short-circuit and ground-fault protection?
master.explanation: Per 430.62(A), the feeder protection is sized based on the largest branch-circuit protective device plus the sum of the other motors' FLCs. First, find FLCs from Table 430.250: 50 HP wound-rotor is 65A, 25 HP is 34A. Next, size the largest branch OCPD (the wound-rotor). Table 430.52 allows a 150% multiplier for a wound-rotor motor's IT breaker: 65A × 1.50 = 97.5A. Per 430.52(C)(1) Exception No. 1, you can use the next standard size up, which is 100A. Finally, add the FLC of the other motors: 100A + 34A + 34A = 168A. Per 430.62(A) Exception, since 168A is not a standard size, you are permitted to use the next higher standard size, which is 175A.
master.sparkyTip: Sizing feeder protection for a group of motors is tricky. It's not just the sum of the branch breakers! Size the largest branch device first (rounding up if needed), then add the other motors' FLCs, and round up again if necessary.

master.questionText: You are installing a 15 HP, 460V, 3-phase high-efficiency motor with a nameplate full-load amperes (FLA) of 18A. The motor has a service factor of 1.15 and a temperature rise of 40°C. What are the minimum required conductor ampacity and the maximum permitted overload protection setting?
master.explanation:
This tests the distinction between 'Table FLC' and 'Nameplate FLA'. 

1) Conductors (430.6(A)(1)): You MUST use the FLC from Table 430.250. A 15 HP, 460V, 3-phase motor is rated at 21A. The conductor ampacity is 125% of this value: 21A × 1.25 = 26.25A. 

2) Overload (430.32(A)(1)): You MUST use the actual Nameplate FLA of 18A. Because the Service Factor is 1.15 and Temp Rise is 40°C, you are permitted to size the overload at 125%: 18A × 1.25 = 22.5A.
master.sparkyTip: Remember the mantra: 'Table for Wires, Nameplate for Heaters (Overloads).' If you swap them, you will calculate 22.5A for the wires, which is undersized!

master.questionText: A feeder supplies three identical 25 HP, 460V, 3-phase, Design B motors. Per Table 430.250, the FLC for each is 34A. What is the minimum required ampacity for the feeder conductors?
master.explanation: Per NEC 430.24, the feeder ampacity is 125% of the FLC of the LARGEST motor, plus the sum of the FLCs of all other motors. Even though they are all the same size, this rule applies to only ONE of them. Calculation: (1.25 × 34A) + 34A + 34A = 42.5A + 68A = 110.5A.
master.sparkyTip: A common mistake is to apply the 125% multiplier to all identical motors. Remember, it only applies to the single 'largest' one, even if they're all the same size.

master.questionText: A hermetic motor-compressor for an A/C unit has a nameplate Rated Load Current (RLC) of 24A and is protected by a separate overload device. What is the maximum permitted trip setting for the overload device?
master.explanation: This is a hermetic motor, so the rules of Article 440 apply, not Article 430. Per NEC 440.52(A)(1), overload relays for hermetic motors shall be selected to trip at not more than 140% of the motor-compressor rated-load current. Calculation: 24A × 1.40 = 33.6A.
master.sparkyTip: Hermetic motors (A/C units) live in Article 440. Their overload protection rules are different from standard motors—140% of RLC, not 115% or 125% of FLC.

master.questionText: A 75 kVA, 480V-208V, 3-phase transformer primary supplies a motor control center. What is the maximum standard size primary circuit breaker allowed if secondary protection is not provided at the transformer?
master.explanation: First, calculate primary FLC: 75,000 VA / (480V × 1.732) = 90.2A. Per NEC 450.3(B), if there is no secondary protection, the primary OCPD can be sized at 125% of the primary FLC. Calculation: 90.2A × 1.25 = 112.75A. Exception No. 1 to 450.3(B) allows rounding up to the next standard size. The next standard size from 112.75A is 125A.
master.sparkyTip: Transformer primary protection is tricky. The default is 125% of primary FLC, but the 'next size up' rule is often permitted, which is a critical exception to know.

master.questionText: A motor control circuit is tapped from a 100A branch circuit inside a motor starter. The control circuit uses #14 AWG copper wire and extends to a remote push-button station. What is the maximum supplementary overcurrent protection required for this control circuit?
master.explanation: Per NEC 430.72(B)(2), motor control conductors extending beyond the enclosure require protection. According to Table 430.72(B), Column B, a #14 AWG copper conductor requires protection of no more than 45A. Since the 100A branch OCPD is too large, separate supplementary protection is required.
master.sparkyTip: Don't forget to protect your control circuit wires! The rules are in Table 430.72(B) and depend on the wire size, not just the motor size.

master.questionText: A motor feeder utilizes conductors with an ampacity of 200A, protected by a larger inverse-time circuit breaker. A 20-foot tap is made from this feeder to supply a single 40 HP, 460V, 3-phase motor (FLC 52A). What is the minimum ampacity required for the tap conductors?
master.explanation: This is a motor feeder tap between 10 and 25 feet, governed by 430.28(2). The tap conductors must have an ampacity of at least 1/3 of the feeder conductors (200A / 3 = 66.7A) AND must have an ampacity for the motor load per Part II of Article 430. A single motor requires conductors at 125% of FLC (52A × 1.25 = 65A). The tap must meet BOTH conditions, so it must have an ampacity of at least 66.7A.
master.sparkyTip: Watch out for the '1/3 Rule' trap! Standard taps (240.21(B)) base the 1/3 calculation on the feeder BREAKER. But motor taps (430.28) base it on the feeder CONDUCTORS. This is because motor breakers are often heavily oversized for inrush current!

master.questionText: An electrician is installing a 1.5 HP, 120V stationary motor and wants to use a standard 20-ampere, AC-only general-use snap switch as the required disconnecting means. According to the NEC, what is the maximum allowable full-load current (FLC) of the motor to legally use this switch?
master.explanation: Step 1: Identify the rule for small stationary motors (2 HP or less). Step 2: Because the prompt specifies an AC-only general-use snap switch, we use 430.109(C)(2). This rule states the motor FLC cannot be more than 80% of the ampere rating of the switch. Step 3: Calculate the maximum FLC: 20A × 0.80 = 16 Amperes.
master.sparkyTip: Read carefully! If the question says 'AC snap switch', multiply the switch rating by 80% (20A switch = 16A motor). But if the question just says 'general-use switch' (430.109(C)(1)), the switch has to be TWICE the size of the motor FLC (20A switch = 10A motor). The wording makes a 6-amp difference!
